a.
$${\left|x-{1\over2}\right|<3}$$
b.
$${\left|2x\right|>{7\over2}}$$
c.
$${\left|{1\over3}x\right|-4<-1}$$

Problem 2
Your favorite dog groomer charges according to your dog’s weight. If your dog is 15 pounds and under, the groomer charges $35. If your dog is between 15 and 40 pounds, she charges $40. If your dog is over 40 pounds, she charges $40, plus an additional $2 for each pound.
Write a piecewise function that describes what your dog groomer charges.
Graph the function.

Graph function
$${f(x)}$$
using a table of values. Define the domain and range of the function.
$${f(x)}=|x+2|-5$$

Solve each absolute value inequality algebraically and match it to a solution or graph shown on the right.

Your summer job pays you $8 for the first 20 hours you work and then time and a half for the next 10 hours you work. If you work more than 30 hours, you do not get paid any more.
Write a piecewise function that models your summer pay possibilities.
Graph your piecewise function.

khan_exercises.title: Graphs of square and cube root functions
khan_exercises.description: Given the formula of a square-root or a cube-root function, find the appropriate graph.
topic_path: Graphs of square and cube root functions
Graphs of square and cube root functions

items.question_content:
The graph of $y=\sqrt{x}$ is shown below.

![The graph of the square root of x on the x y plane. It starts at (0, 0) and increases through the points (1, 1), (4, 2), and (9, 3).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c4d447646a783c7fc85a27e5d31d21541a7be7d7)

**Which of the following is the graph of $y=-\sqrt{x+3}-5$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The 
graph of $y=\sqrt{x}$ can be transformed
to get the graph of $y=-\sqrt{x+3}-5$. 
[[☃ explanation 1]]

Replacing $x$ with $x+3$ shifts the graph of $y=\sqrt{x}$ to the left $3$ units.

![The graph of two square root functions. One is the graph of the square root of x. It starts at (0, 0) and increases through the points (1, 1), (4, 2), and (9, 3). The other is the graph of the square root of x shifted to the left 3 units. It starts at (negative 3, 0) and increases through the points (negative 2, 1), (1, 2), and (6, 3). ](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c9124f621473c33e7eb4bd7c2bc7523717b72685)

Multiplying $\sqrt{x+3}$ by $-1$ reflects the graph of $y=\sqrt{x+3}$ over the $x$-axis.

![A square root function graph and its vertically reflected graph on an x y coordinate plane. It has an endpoint (negative 3, zero) and passes through (one, two). The vertically reflected graph has an endpoint (negative three, zero) and passes through (one, negative two).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8b582df7f8374048661d52543b3ed38ed1df2778)

Adding $-5$ to the function shifts the graph of $y=-\sqrt{x+3}$ down $5$ units.

![A square root function graph and its shifted graph on an x y coordinate plane. It has an endpoint (negative 3, zero) and passes through (one, negative two). The shifted graph has an endpoint (negative three, negative five) and passes through (one, negative seven).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/acb9d6efad3d717a391da666a7c1c8c4f2810257)

Note that it is important to follow order of operations when building the function, as function transformations are not always commutative.  [[☃ explanation 2]]

**The graph that corresponds with these transformations is graph $A$:**

![A square root function graph on an x y coordinate plane. It is shifted three units to the left, five units down and vertically reflected.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7746e7dec8e560e8a4924a211dca36e662d1e0cd)

![A square root function graph on an x y coordinate plane. It is shifted three units to the right, five units down and horizontally reflected.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/52df8daa3a33f1663c40d363b7dac11021bb84b4)

The graph of $y=\sqrt{x}$ is shown below.

![The graph of the square root of x on the x y plane. It starts at (0, 0) and increases through the points (1, 1), (4, 2), and (9, 3).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c4d447646a783c7fc85a27e5d31d21541a7be7d7)

**Which of the following is the graph of $y=3\sqrt{2-x}$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The graph of $y=\sqrt{x}$ can be transformed
to get the graph of $y=3\sqrt{2-x}$. 
[[☃ explanation 1]]

Let's write $y=3\sqrt{2-x}$ as $y=3\sqrt{-(x-2)}$ so that it is easier to see the transformations from the graph of $y=\sqrt{x}$.  
Replacing $x$ with $x-2$ shifts the graph of $y=\sqrt{x}$ to the right $2$ units.


[[☃ image 1]]

Multiplying $x-2$ by $-1$ reflects the graph of $y=\sqrt{x-2}$ over the line $x=2$.



[[☃ image 1]]

Multiplying $\sqrt{-(x-2)}$ by $3$ stretches the graph of $y=\sqrt{-(x-2)} $ vertically by a factor of $3$.  



[[☃ image 1]]

Note that the graph of the function passes through $(-2,2)$ *before* the stretch, and $(-2, 6 )$ *after* the stretch.  This is because each $y$-coordinate is multiplied by $3$.  

Additionally it is important to follow order of operations when building the function, as function transformations are not always commutative.  [[☃ explanation 2]]
**The graph that corresponds with these transformations is graph $B$:**

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following is the graph of $y=-3\sqrt{x-1}+2$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The graph of $y=\sqrt{x}$ can be transformed
to get the graph of $y=-3\sqrt{x-1}+2$. 
[[☃ explanation 1]]

Replacing $x$ with $x-1$ shifts the graph of $y=\sqrt{x}$ to the right $1$ unit.

[[☃ image 1]]

Multiplying $\sqrt{x-1}$ by $-3$ reflects the graph of $y=\sqrt{x-1}$ across the $x$-axis and stretches it vertically by a factor of $3$.

[[☃ image 1]]

Note that the graph of the function passes through $(5,2)$ *before* the transformation, and $(5, -6 )$ *after* the transformation.  This is because each $y$-coordinate is multiplied by $-3$.

Adding $2$ to the function shifts the graph of $y=-3\sqrt{x-1}$ up $2$ units.

[[☃ image 1]]

Note that it is important to follow order of operations when building the function, as function transformations are not always commutative.  [[☃ explanation 2]]

**The graph that corresponds with these transformations is graph $C$:**

[[☃ image 1]]

khan_exercises.title: Definite integrals: common functions
khan_exercises.description: Evaluate definite integrals with a variety of functions including rational, square root, cube root, exponential, and natural logs.
topic_path: Finding antiderivatives and integrals: basic rules and notation: definite integrals

items.question_content:
**Evaluate the definite integral.**

$\displaystyle\int^2_{1}\left(\dfrac{12}{x^2}-1\right)\,dx = $ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
First, use the power rule:

$\begin{align}\displaystyle\int^2_{1}\left(\dfrac{12}{x^2}-1\right)\,dx~&=\displaystyle\int^2_{1}\left(12x^{-2}-1\right)\,dx
\\&=~(-12x^{-1}-x)\Bigg|^\blueD2_{\maroonD1}\end{align}$

Second, plug in the limits of integration:

$[-12\cdot\blueD2^{-1}-\blueD2]-[-12\cdot\maroonD1^{-1}-\maroonD1] = -8+13 = 5$.

The answer:

$\displaystyle\int^2_{1}\left(\dfrac{12}{x^2}-1\right)\,dx = 5$

items.question_content:
**Evaluate the definite integral.**

$\displaystyle\int^{-2}_{-1}\left(\dfrac{16-x^3}{x^3}\right)\,dx = $ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
First, use the power rule:

$\begin{align}\displaystyle\int^{-2}_{-1}\left(\dfrac{16-x^3}{x^3}\right)\,dx~&=~\displaystyle\int^{-2}_{-1}\left(\dfrac{16}{x^3}-\dfrac{x^3}{x^3}\right)\,dx
\\&=~\displaystyle\int^{-2}_{-1}\left(16{x^{-3}}-{1}\right)\,dx
\\&=(-8x^{-2}-x)\Bigg|^\blueD{-2}_{\maroonD{-1}}\end{align}$

Second, plug in the limits of integration:

$[-8\cdot(\blueD{-2})^{-2}-(\blueD{-2})]-[-8\cdot(\maroonD{-1})^{-2}-(\maroonD{-1})] = 0+7 = 7$.

The answer:

$\displaystyle\int^{-2}_{-1}\left(\dfrac{16-x^3}{x^3}\right)\,dx= 7$

items.question_content:
**Evaluate the definite integral.**

$\displaystyle\int^{-3}_{-1}\left(\dfrac{27}{x^4}-3\right)\,dx = $ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
First, use the power rule:

$\begin{align}\displaystyle\int^{-3}_{-1}\left(\dfrac{27}{x^4}-3\right)\,dx~&=~\displaystyle\int^{-3}_{-1}\left({27}{x^{-4}}-3\right)\,dx
\\&=(-9x^{-3}-3x)\Bigg|^\blueD{-3}_{\maroonD{-1}}\end{align}$

Second, plug in the limits of integration:

$[-9\cdot(\blueD{-3})^{-3}-3(\blueD{-3})]-[-9\cdot(\maroonD{-1})^{-3}-3\cdot(\maroonD{-1})] = \dfrac{28}{3}-12 = -\dfrac{8}{3}$.

The answer:

$\displaystyle\int^{-3}_{-1}\left(\dfrac{27}{x^4}-3\right)\,dx = -\dfrac{8}{3}$

items.question_content:
**Evaluate the definite integral.**

$\displaystyle\int^4_{3}\left(4+\dfrac{24}{x^2}\right)\,dx = $ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
First, use the power rule:

$\begin{align}\displaystyle\int^4_{3}\left(4+\dfrac{24}{x^2}\right)\,dx~&= \displaystyle\int^4_{3}\left(4+24x^{-2}\right)\,dx
\\&=
~(4x-24x^{-1})\Bigg|^\blueD4_{\maroonD3}\end{align}$

Second, plug in the limits of integration:

$[4\cdot\blueD4-24\cdot\blueD4^{-1}]-[4\cdot\maroonD3-24\cdot\maroonD3^{-1}] = 10-4 = 6$.

The answer:

$\displaystyle\int^4_{3}\left(4+\dfrac{24}{x^2}\right)\,dx = 6$

items.question_content:
**Evaluate the definite integral.**

$\displaystyle\int^{-1}_{-5}\left(\dfrac{x^6-25x^4}{x^6}\right)\,dx = $ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
First, use the power rule:

$\begin{align}\displaystyle\int^{-1}_{-5}\left(\dfrac{x^6-25x^4}{x^6}\right)\,dx~&=~\displaystyle\int^{-1}_{-5}\left(\dfrac{x^6}{x^6}-\dfrac{25x^4}{x^6}\right)\,dx
\\&=~\displaystyle\int^{-1}_{-5}\left(1-25x^{-2}\right)\,dx
\\&=(x+25x^{-1})\Bigg|^\blueD{-1}_{\maroonD{-5}}\end{align}$

Second, plug in the limits of integration:

$[\blueD{-1}+25\cdot(\blueD{-1})^{-1}]-[\maroonD{-5}+25\cdot(\maroonD{-5})^{-1}] = -26+10 = -16$.

The answer:

$\displaystyle\int^{-1}_{-5}\left(\dfrac{x^6-25x^4}{x^6}\right)\,dx= -16$

items.question_content:
**Evaluate the definite integral.**

$\displaystyle\int^{9}_{5}\left(\dfrac{x-4}{x^2}\right)\,dx = $ 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
First, simplify and use the power and natural log rules:

$\begin{align}\displaystyle\int^{9}_{5}\left(\dfrac{x-4}{x^2}\right)\,dx ~&=~\displaystyle\int^{9}_{5}\left(\dfrac{x}{x^2}-\dfrac{4}{x^2}\right)\,dx
\\&=~\displaystyle\int^{9}_{5}\left(\dfrac{1}{x}-4x^{-2}\right)\,dx
\\&=\left(\ln(x)+4x^{-1}\right)\Bigg|^\blueD{9}_{\maroonD5}\end{align}$

Second, plug in the limits of integration:

$(\ln(\blueD{9})+4\cdot\blueD{9}^{-1})-(\ln(\maroonD{5})+4\cdot\maroonD{5}^{-1}) = \ln\left(\dfrac95\right)-\dfrac{16}{45}$.

The answer:

$\displaystyle\int^{9}_{5}\left(\dfrac{x-4}{x^2}\right)\,dx ~=~\ln\left(\dfrac95\right)-\dfrac{16}{45}$

choices.content: $\ln\left(\dfrac95\right)-\dfrac{16}{45}$
$\ln\left(4\right)+\dfrac{16}{45}$
None of the above

items.question_content:
**Evaluate the definite integral.**

$\displaystyle\int^{4}_{2}\left(\dfrac{6+x^2}{x^3}\right)\,dx = $ 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
First, simplify and use the power and natural log rules:

$\begin{align}\displaystyle\int^{4}_{2}\left(\dfrac{6+x^2}{x^3}\right)\,dx ~&=~\displaystyle\int^{4}_{2}\left(\dfrac{6}{x^3}+\dfrac{x^2}{x^3}\right)\,dx
\\&=~\displaystyle\int^{4}_{2}\left(6x^{-3}+\dfrac{1}{x}\right)\,dx
\\&=\left(-3x^{-2}+\ln(x)\right)\Bigg|^\blueD{4}_{\maroonD2}\end{align}$

Second, plug in the limits of integration:

$(\ln(\blueD{4})-3\cdot\blueD{4}^{-2})-(\ln(\maroonD{2})-3\cdot\maroonD{2}^{-2}) = \ln\left(2\right)+\dfrac{9}{16}$.

The answer:

$\displaystyle\int^{4}_{2}\left(\dfrac{6+x^2}{x^3}\right)\,dx ~=~\ln\left(2\right)+\dfrac{9}{16}$

choices.content: $\ln\left(2\right)+\dfrac{9}{16}$
$-\ln\left(2\right)+\dfrac{3}{16}$
None of the above

items.question_content:
**Evaluate the definite integral.**

$\displaystyle\int^{5}_{2}\left(\dfrac{3x+3}{x^2}\right)\,dx = $ 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
First, simplify and use the power and natural log rules:

$\begin{align}\displaystyle\int^{5}_{2}\left(\dfrac{3x+3}{x^2}\right)\,dx ~&=~\displaystyle\int^{5}_{2}\left(\dfrac{3x}{x^2}+\dfrac{3}{x^2}\right)\,dx
\\&=~\displaystyle\int^{5}_{2}\left(\dfrac{3}{x}+3x^{-2}\right)\,dx
\\&=\left(3\ln(x)-3x^{-1}\right)\Bigg|^\blueD{5}_{\maroonD2}\end{align}$

Second, plug in the limits of integration:

$(3\ln(\blueD{5})-3\cdot\blueD{5}^{-1})-(3\ln(\maroonD{2})-3\cdot\maroonD{2}^{-1}) = 3\ln\left(\dfrac52\right)+\dfrac{9}{10}$.

The answer:

$\displaystyle\int^{5}_{2}\left(\dfrac{3x+3}{x^2}\right)\,dx~=~3\ln\left(\dfrac52\right)+\dfrac{9}{10}$

choices.content: $\ln\left(\dfrac{15}2\right)-\dfrac{9}{10}$
$3\ln\left(\dfrac52\right)+\dfrac{9}{10}$
None of the above

items.question_content:
**Evaluate the definite integral.**

$\displaystyle\int^{3}_{1}\left(\dfrac{15+2x^3}{x^4}\right)\,dx = $ 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
First, simplify and use the power and natural log rules:

$\begin{align}\displaystyle\int^{3}_{1}\left(\dfrac{15+2x^3}{x^4}\right)\,dx ~&=~\displaystyle\int^{3}_{1}\left(\dfrac{15}{x^4}+\dfrac{2x^3}{x^4}\right)\,dx
\\&=~\displaystyle\int^{3}_{1}\left(15x^{-4}+\dfrac{2}{x}\right)\,dx
\\&=\left(-5x^{-3}+2\ln(x)\right)\Bigg|^\blueD{3}_{\maroonD{1}}\end{align}$

Second, plug in the limits of integration:

$(2\ln(\blueD{3})-5\cdot\blueD{3}^{-3})-(2\ln(\maroonD{1})-5\cdot\maroonD{1}^{-3}) = 2\ln\left(3\right)+\dfrac{130}{27}$.

The answer:

$\displaystyle\int^{3}_{1}\left(\dfrac{15+2x^3}{x^4}\right)\,dx ~=~2\ln\left(3\right)+\dfrac{130}{27}$

$2\ln\left(3\right)+\dfrac{130}{27}$
None of the above

khan_exercises.title: Graphs of square and cube root functions
khan_exercises.description: Given the formula of a square-root or a cube-root function, find the appropriate graph.
topic_path: Module 4: Polynomial and quadratic expressions, equations, and functions
Topic C: Lessons 20-22: Scaling and transforming graphs
Graphs of square and cube root functions

items.question_content:
The graph of $y=\sqrt{x}$ is shown below.

![The graph of the square root of x on the x y plane. It starts at (0, 0) and increases through the points (1, 1), (4, 2), and (9, 3).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c4d447646a783c7fc85a27e5d31d21541a7be7d7)

**Which of the following is the graph of $y=-\sqrt{x+3}-5$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The 
graph of $y=\sqrt{x}$ can be transformed
to get the graph of $y=-\sqrt{x+3}-5$. 
[[☃ explanation 1]]

Replacing $x$ with $x+3$ shifts the graph of $y=\sqrt{x}$ to the left $3$ units.

![The graph of two square root functions. One is the graph of the square root of x. It starts at (0, 0) and increases through the points (1, 1), (4, 2), and (9, 3). The other is the graph of the square root of x shifted to the left 3 units. It starts at (negative 3, 0) and increases through the points (negative 2, 1), (1, 2), and (6, 3). ](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c9124f621473c33e7eb4bd7c2bc7523717b72685)

Multiplying $\sqrt{x+3}$ by $-1$ reflects the graph of $y=\sqrt{x+3}$ over the $x$-axis.

![A square root function graph and its vertically reflected graph on an x y coordinate plane. It has an endpoint (negative 3, zero) and passes through (one, two). The vertically reflected graph has an endpoint (negative three, zero) and passes through (one, negative two).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8b582df7f8374048661d52543b3ed38ed1df2778)

Adding $-5$ to the function shifts the graph of $y=-\sqrt{x+3}$ down $5$ units.

![A square root function graph and its shifted graph on an x y coordinate plane. It has an endpoint (negative 3, zero) and passes through (one, negative two). The shifted graph has an endpoint (negative three, negative five) and passes through (one, negative seven).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/acb9d6efad3d717a391da666a7c1c8c4f2810257)

Note that it is important to follow order of operations when building the function, as function transformations are not always commutative.  [[☃ explanation 2]]

**The graph that corresponds with these transformations is graph $A$:**

![A square root function graph on an x y coordinate plane. It is shifted three units to the left, five units down and vertically reflected.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7746e7dec8e560e8a4924a211dca36e662d1e0cd)

![A square root function graph on an x y coordinate plane. It is shifted three units to the right, five units down and horizontally reflected.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/52df8daa3a33f1663c40d363b7dac11021bb84b4)

![A square root function graph on an x y coordinate plane. It is shifted three units to the left, five units down and horizontally reflected.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a8376c37dfb7271343e3e4f937abb071f4d0e971)

![A square root function graph on an x y coordinate plane. It is shifted three units to the right, 5 units down and vertically reflected.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e200eb43b9ac128ef3e1dee30c52cc19538384da)

The graph of $y=\sqrt{x}$ is shown below.

![The graph of the square root of x on the x y plane. It starts at (0, 0) and increases through the points (1, 1), (4, 2), and (9, 3).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c4d447646a783c7fc85a27e5d31d21541a7be7d7)

**Which of the following is the graph of $y=3\sqrt{2-x}$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The graph of $y=\sqrt{x}$ can be transformed
to get the graph of $y=3\sqrt{2-x}$. 
[[☃ explanation 1]]

Let's write $y=3\sqrt{2-x}$ as $y=3\sqrt{-(x-2)}$ so that it is easier to see the transformations from the graph of $y=\sqrt{x}$.  
Replacing $x$ with $x-2$ shifts the graph of $y=\sqrt{x}$ to the right $2$ units.


[[☃ image 1]]

Multiplying $x-2$ by $-1$ reflects the graph of $y=\sqrt{x-2}$ over the line $x=2$.



[[☃ image 1]]

Multiplying $\sqrt{-(x-2)}$ by $3$ stretches the graph of $y=\sqrt{-(x-2)} $ vertically by a factor of $3$.  



[[☃ image 1]]

Note that the graph of the function passes through $(-2,2)$ *before* the stretch, and $(-2, 6 )$ *after* the stretch.  This is because each $y$-coordinate is multiplied by $3$.  

Additionally it is important to follow order of operations when building the function, as function transformations are not always commutative.  [[☃ explanation 2]]
**The graph that corresponds with these transformations is graph $B$:**

[[☃ image 1]]

![A square root function graph on an x y coordinate plane. It is shifted two units to the right, horizontally reflected, and stretched vertically by a scale factor of three.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9c3dec5a8719a7d7ea5669d5dbfe7d8e0b71735e)

![A cube root function graph on an x y coordinate plane. It has a middle point at (zero, zero) and passes through (negative eight, negative two) and (eight, two). ](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/afeb9a9d7e4c7c5089f71d0624a6146c529ef093)

**Which of the following is the graph of $y=3\sqrt[3]{x+6}$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{x}$ can be transformed
to get the graph of $y=3\sqrt[3]{x+6}$. 
[[☃ explanation 1]]

Replacing $x$ with $x+6$ shifts the graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{x}$ left $6$ units.

[[☃ image 1]]

Multiplying $\sqrt[3]{x+6}$ by $3$ stretches the graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{x+6} $ vertically by a factor of $3$.  

[[☃ image 1]]

Note that the graph of the function passes through $(2,2)$ *before* the stretch, and $(2, 6 )$ *after* the stretch.  This is because each $y$-coordinate is multiplied by $3$.  

Additionally it is important to follow order of operations when building the function, as function transformations are not always commutative.  [[☃ explanation 1]]

**The graph that corresponds with these transformations is graph $B$:**

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following is the graph of $y=-2\sqrt[3]{x-3}+2$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{x}$ can be transformed
to get the graph of $y=-2\sqrt[3]{x-3}+2$. 
[[☃ explanation 1]]

Replacing $x$ with $x-3$ shifts the graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{x}$ right $3$ units.


[[☃ image 1]]

Multiplying $\sqrt[3]{x-3}$ by $-2$ reflects the graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{x-3}$ across the $x$-axis and stretches it vertically by a factor of $2$.

[[☃ image 1]]

Note that the graph of the function passes through $(2,-1)$ *before* the transformation, and $(2, 2 )$ *after* the transformation.  This is because each $y$-coordinate is multiplied by $-2$.

Adding $2$ to the function shifts the graph of $y=-2\sqrt[3]{x-3}$ up $2$ units.

[[☃ image 1]]

Note that it is important to follow order of operations when building the function, as function transformations are not always commutative.  [[☃ explanation 1]]

**The graph that corresponds with these transformations is graph $C$:**

[[☃ image 1]]

Adding $-5$ to the function shifts the graph of $y=-3\sqrt[3]{-x}$ down $5$ units.

khan_exercises.title: Definite integrals: common functions
khan_exercises.description: Evaluate definite integrals with a variety of functions including rational, square root, cube root, exponential, and natural logs.
topic_path: Finding antiderivatives and indefinite integrals: basic rules and notation: definite integrals

items.question_content:
**Evaluate the definite integral.**

$\displaystyle\int^2_{1}\left(\dfrac{12}{x^2}-1\right)\,dx = $ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
First, use the power rule:

$\begin{align}\displaystyle\int^2_{1}\left(\dfrac{12}{x^2}-1\right)\,dx~&=\displaystyle\int^2_{1}\left(12x^{-2}-1\right)\,dx
\\&=~(-12x^{-1}-x)\Bigg|^\blueD2_{\maroonD1}\end{align}$

Second, plug in the limits of integration:

$[-12\cdot\blueD2^{-1}-\blueD2]-[-12\cdot\maroonD1^{-1}-\maroonD1] = -8+13 = 5$.

The answer:

$\displaystyle\int^2_{1}\left(\dfrac{12}{x^2}-1\right)\,dx = 5$

items.question_content:
**Evaluate the definite integral.**

$\displaystyle\int^{-2}_{-1}\left(\dfrac{16-x^3}{x^3}\right)\,dx = $ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
First, use the power rule:

$\begin{align}\displaystyle\int^{-2}_{-1}\left(\dfrac{16-x^3}{x^3}\right)\,dx~&=~\displaystyle\int^{-2}_{-1}\left(\dfrac{16}{x^3}-\dfrac{x^3}{x^3}\right)\,dx
\\&=~\displaystyle\int^{-2}_{-1}\left(16{x^{-3}}-{1}\right)\,dx
\\&=(-8x^{-2}-x)\Bigg|^\blueD{-2}_{\maroonD{-1}}\end{align}$

Second, plug in the limits of integration:

$[-8\cdot(\blueD{-2})^{-2}-(\blueD{-2})]-[-8\cdot(\maroonD{-1})^{-2}-(\maroonD{-1})] = 0+7 = 7$.

The answer:

$\displaystyle\int^{-2}_{-1}\left(\dfrac{16-x^3}{x^3}\right)\,dx= 7$

items.question_content:
**Evaluate the definite integral.**

$\displaystyle\int^{-3}_{-1}\left(\dfrac{27}{x^4}-3\right)\,dx = $ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
First, use the power rule:

$\begin{align}\displaystyle\int^{-3}_{-1}\left(\dfrac{27}{x^4}-3\right)\,dx~&=~\displaystyle\int^{-3}_{-1}\left({27}{x^{-4}}-3\right)\,dx
\\&=(-9x^{-3}-3x)\Bigg|^\blueD{-3}_{\maroonD{-1}}\end{align}$

Second, plug in the limits of integration:

$[-9\cdot(\blueD{-3})^{-3}-3(\blueD{-3})]-[-9\cdot(\maroonD{-1})^{-3}-3\cdot(\maroonD{-1})] = \dfrac{28}{3}-12 = -\dfrac{8}{3}$.

The answer:

$\displaystyle\int^{-3}_{-1}\left(\dfrac{27}{x^4}-3\right)\,dx = -\dfrac{8}{3}$

items.question_content:
**Evaluate the definite integral.**

$\displaystyle\int^4_{3}\left(4+\dfrac{24}{x^2}\right)\,dx = $ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
First, use the power rule:

$\begin{align}\displaystyle\int^4_{3}\left(4+\dfrac{24}{x^2}\right)\,dx~&= \displaystyle\int^4_{3}\left(4+24x^{-2}\right)\,dx
\\&=
~(4x-24x^{-1})\Bigg|^\blueD4_{\maroonD3}\end{align}$

Second, plug in the limits of integration:

$[4\cdot\blueD4-24\cdot\blueD4^{-1}]-[4\cdot\maroonD3-24\cdot\maroonD3^{-1}] = 10-4 = 6$.

The answer:

$\displaystyle\int^4_{3}\left(4+\dfrac{24}{x^2}\right)\,dx = 6$

items.question_content:
**Evaluate the definite integral.**

$\displaystyle\int^{-1}_{-5}\left(\dfrac{x^6-25x^4}{x^6}\right)\,dx = $ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
First, use the power rule:

$\begin{align}\displaystyle\int^{-1}_{-5}\left(\dfrac{x^6-25x^4}{x^6}\right)\,dx~&=~\displaystyle\int^{-1}_{-5}\left(\dfrac{x^6}{x^6}-\dfrac{25x^4}{x^6}\right)\,dx
\\&=~\displaystyle\int^{-1}_{-5}\left(1-25x^{-2}\right)\,dx
\\&=(x+25x^{-1})\Bigg|^\blueD{-1}_{\maroonD{-5}}\end{align}$

Second, plug in the limits of integration:

$[\blueD{-1}+25\cdot(\blueD{-1})^{-1}]-[\maroonD{-5}+25\cdot(\maroonD{-5})^{-1}] = -26+10 = -16$.

The answer:

$\displaystyle\int^{-1}_{-5}\left(\dfrac{x^6-25x^4}{x^6}\right)\,dx= -16$

items.question_content:
**Evaluate the definite integral.**

$\displaystyle\int^{16}_{4}\left(3\sqrt{x}\right)\,dx = $ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
First, use the power rule:

$\begin{align}\displaystyle\int^{16}_{4}\left(3\sqrt{x}\right)\,dx ~&=~\displaystyle\int^{16}_{4}\left(3x^{\frac12}\right)\,dx
\\&=(2x^\frac32)\Bigg|^\blueD{16}_{\maroonD4}\end{align}$

Second, plug in the limits of integration:

$(2\cdot\blueD{16}^{\frac32})-(2\cdot\maroonD4^{\frac32}) = 128-16 = 112$.

The answer:

$\displaystyle\int^{16}_{4}\left(3\sqrt{x}\right)\,dx ~=~112$

items.question_content:
**Evaluate the definite integral.**

$\displaystyle\int^{25}_{0}\left(-6\sqrt{x}\right)\,dx = $ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
First, use the power rule:

$\begin{align}\displaystyle\int^{25}_{0}\left(-6\sqrt{x}\right)\,dx ~&=~\displaystyle\int^{25}_{0}\left(-6x^{\frac12}\right)\,dx
\\&=(-4x^\frac32)\Bigg|^\blueD{25}_{\maroonD0}\end{align}$

Second, plug in the limits of integration:

$(-4\cdot\blueD{25}^{\frac32})-(-4\cdot\maroonD0^{\frac32}) = -500+0 = -500$.

The answer:

$\displaystyle\int^{25}_{0}\left(-6\sqrt{x}\right)\,dx ~=~-500$

Second, plug in the limits of integration:

$(-10\cdot\blueD{1}^{\frac32})-(-10\cdot\maroonD{9}^{\frac32}) = -10+270 = 260$.

The answer:

Second, plug in the limits of integration:

items.question_content:
**Evaluate the definite integral.**

$\displaystyle\int^{27}_{8}\left(\frac43\sqrt[3]{x}\right)\,dx = $ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
First, use the power rule:

$\begin{align}\displaystyle\int^{27}_{8}\left(\frac43\sqrt[3]{x}\right)\,dx~&=~\displaystyle\int^{27}_{8}\left(\frac43x^\frac13\right)\,dx
\\&=(x^\frac43)\Bigg|^\blueD{27}_{\maroonD8}\end{align}$

Second, plug in the limits of integration:

$[\blueD{27}^{\frac43}]-[\maroonD8^{\frac43}] = 81-16 = 65$.

The answer:

$\displaystyle\int^{27}_{8}\left(\frac43\sqrt[3]{x}\right)\,dx~=~65$

items.question_content:
**Evaluate the definite integral.**

$\displaystyle\int^{1}_{-1}\left(12\sqrt[3]{x}\right)\,dx = $ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]

$\begin{align}\displaystyle\int^{1}_{-1}\left(12\sqrt[3]{x}\right)\,dx~&=~\displaystyle\int^{1}_{-1}\left(12x^\frac13\right)\,dx

Second, plug in the limits of integration:

$[9\cdot\blueD{1}^{\frac43}]-[9\cdot(\maroonD{-1})^{\frac43}] = 9-9 = 0$.

The answer:

$\displaystyle\int^{1}_{-1}\left(12\sqrt[3]{x}\right)\,dx~=~0$

$[-3\cdot(\blueD{-8})^{\frac43}]-[-3\cdot\maroonD0^{\frac43}] = -48-0 = -48$.

items.question_content:
**Evaluate the definite integral.**

$\displaystyle\int^{4\pi}_{3\pi}2\sin(x)\,dx = $ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
First, use the sine rule:

$\begin{align}\displaystyle\int^{4\pi}_{3\pi}2\sin(x)\,dx~&=~-2\cos(x)\Bigg|^\blueD{4\pi}_{\maroonD{3\pi}}\end{align}$

Second, plug in the limits of integration:

$(-2\cdot\blueD{\cos(4\pi)})-(-2\cdot\maroonD{\cos(3\pi)}) = -2-2 =-4$.

The answer:

$\displaystyle\int^{4\pi}_{3\pi}2\sin(x)\,dx~=~-4$

items.question_content:
**Evaluate the definite integral.**

$\displaystyle\int^{2\pi}_{\pi}-\cos(x)\,dx = $ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
First, use the cosine rule:

$\begin{align}\displaystyle\int^{2\pi}_{\pi}-\cos(x)\,dx~&=~-\sin(x)\Bigg|^\blueD{2\pi}_{\maroonD{\pi}}\end{align}$

Second, plug in the limits of integration:

$(-\blueD{\sin(2\pi)})-(-\maroonD{\sin(\pi)}) = 0-0 =0$.

The answer:

$\displaystyle\int^{2\pi}_{\pi}-\cos(x)\,dx~=~0$

items.question_content:
**Evaluate the definite integral.**

$\displaystyle\int^{2\pi}_{\frac{3\pi}{2}}-4\sin(x)\,dx = $ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
First, use the sine rule:

$\begin{align}\displaystyle\int^{2\pi}_{\frac{3\pi}{2}}-4\sin(x)\,dx~&=~4\cos(x)\Bigg|^\blueD{2\pi}_{\maroonD{\frac{3\pi}{2}}}\end{align}$

Second, plug in the limits of integration:

$(4\cdot\blueD{\cos(2\pi)})-(4\cdot\maroonD{\cos(\frac{3\pi}{2})}) = 4+0 =4$.

The answer:

$\displaystyle\int^{2\pi}_{\frac{3\pi}{2}}-4\sin(x)\,dx~=~4$

Second, plug in the limits of integration:

$(5\cdot\blueD{\sin(-\frac{5\pi}{2})})-(5\cdot\maroonD{\sin(-2\pi)}) = -5-0 =-5$.

The answer:

$\displaystyle\int^{-\frac{5\pi}{2}}_{-2\pi}5\cos(x)\,dx~=~-5$

$\displaystyle\int^{12}_{6}6e^x\,dx = $

items.question_content:
**Evaluate the definite integral.**

$\displaystyle\int^{9}_{5}\left(\dfrac{x-4}{x^2}\right)\,dx = $ 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
First, simplify and use the power and natural log rules:

$\begin{align}\displaystyle\int^{9}_{5}\left(\dfrac{x-4}{x^2}\right)\,dx ~&=~\displaystyle\int^{9}_{5}\left(\dfrac{x}{x^2}-\dfrac{4}{x^2}\right)\,dx
\\&=~\displaystyle\int^{9}_{5}\left(\dfrac{1}{x}-4x^{-2}\right)\,dx
\\&=\left(\ln(x)+4x^{-1}\right)\Bigg|^\blueD{9}_{\maroonD5}\end{align}$

Second, plug in the limits of integration:

$(\ln(\blueD{9})+4\cdot\blueD{9}^{-1})-(\ln(\maroonD{5})+4\cdot\maroonD{5}^{-1}) = \ln\left(\dfrac95\right)-\dfrac{16}{45}$.

The answer:

$\displaystyle\int^{9}_{5}\left(\dfrac{x-4}{x^2}\right)\,dx ~=~\ln\left(\dfrac95\right)-\dfrac{16}{45}$

choices.content: $\ln\left(\dfrac95\right)-\dfrac{16}{45}$
$\ln\left(4\right)+\dfrac{16}{45}$
None of the above

items.question_content:
**Evaluate the definite integral.**

$\displaystyle\int^{4}_{2}\left(\dfrac{6+x^2}{x^3}\right)\,dx = $ 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
First, simplify and use the power and natural log rules:

$\begin{align}\displaystyle\int^{4}_{2}\left(\dfrac{6+x^2}{x^3}\right)\,dx ~&=~\displaystyle\int^{4}_{2}\left(\dfrac{6}{x^3}+\dfrac{x^2}{x^3}\right)\,dx
\\&=~\displaystyle\int^{4}_{2}\left(6x^{-3}+\dfrac{1}{x}\right)\,dx
\\&=\left(-3x^{-2}+\ln(x)\right)\Bigg|^\blueD{4}_{\maroonD2}\end{align}$

Second, plug in the limits of integration:

$(\ln(\blueD{4})-3\cdot\blueD{4}^{-2})-(\ln(\maroonD{2})-3\cdot\maroonD{2}^{-2}) = \ln\left(2\right)+\dfrac{9}{16}$.

The answer:

$\displaystyle\int^{4}_{2}\left(\dfrac{6+x^2}{x^3}\right)\,dx ~=~\ln\left(2\right)+\dfrac{9}{16}$

choices.content: $\ln\left(2\right)+\dfrac{9}{16}$
$-\ln\left(2\right)+\dfrac{3}{16}$
None of the above

items.question_content:
**Evaluate the definite integral.**

$\displaystyle\int^{5}_{2}\left(\dfrac{3x+3}{x^2}\right)\,dx = $ 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
First, simplify and use the power and natural log rules:

$\begin{align}\displaystyle\int^{5}_{2}\left(\dfrac{3x+3}{x^2}\right)\,dx ~&=~\displaystyle\int^{5}_{2}\left(\dfrac{3x}{x^2}+\dfrac{3}{x^2}\right)\,dx
\\&=~\displaystyle\int^{5}_{2}\left(\dfrac{3}{x}+3x^{-2}\right)\,dx
\\&=\left(3\ln(x)-3x^{-1}\right)\Bigg|^\blueD{5}_{\maroonD2}\end{align}$

Second, plug in the limits of integration:

$(3\ln(\blueD{5})-3\cdot\blueD{5}^{-1})-(3\ln(\maroonD{2})-3\cdot\maroonD{2}^{-1}) = 3\ln\left(\dfrac52\right)+\dfrac{9}{10}$.

The answer:

$\displaystyle\int^{5}_{2}\left(\dfrac{3x+3}{x^2}\right)\,dx~=~3\ln\left(\dfrac52\right)+\dfrac{9}{10}$

choices.content: $\ln\left(\dfrac{15}2\right)-\dfrac{9}{10}$
$3\ln\left(\dfrac52\right)+\dfrac{9}{10}$
None of the above

items.question_content:
**Evaluate the definite integral.**

$\displaystyle\int^{3}_{1}\left(\dfrac{15+2x^3}{x^4}\right)\,dx = $ 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
First, simplify and use the power and natural log rules:

$\begin{align}\displaystyle\int^{3}_{1}\left(\dfrac{15+2x^3}{x^4}\right)\,dx ~&=~\displaystyle\int^{3}_{1}\left(\dfrac{15}{x^4}+\dfrac{2x^3}{x^4}\right)\,dx
\\&=~\displaystyle\int^{3}_{1}\left(15x^{-4}+\dfrac{2}{x}\right)\,dx
\\&=\left(-5x^{-3}+2\ln(x)\right)\Bigg|^\blueD{3}_{\maroonD{1}}\end{align}$

Second, plug in the limits of integration:

$(2\ln(\blueD{3})-5\cdot\blueD{3}^{-3})-(2\ln(\maroonD{1})-5\cdot\maroonD{1}^{-3}) = 2\ln\left(3\right)+\dfrac{130}{27}$.

The answer:

$\displaystyle\int^{3}_{1}\left(\dfrac{15+2x^3}{x^4}\right)\,dx ~=~2\ln\left(3\right)+\dfrac{130}{27}$

items.question_content:
**Evaluate the definite integral.**

$\displaystyle\int^{2}_{5}\left(\dfrac{12-x^3}{x^4}\right)\,dx = $ 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
First, simplify and use the power and natural log rules:

\\&=~\displaystyle\int^{2}_{5}\left(12x^{-4}-x^{-1}\right)\,dx
\\&=\left(-4x^{-3}-\ln(x)\right)\Bigg|^\blueD{2}_{\maroonD{5}}\end{align}$

Second, plug in the limits of integration:

$(-\ln(\blueD{2})-4\cdot\blueD{2}^{-3})-(-\ln(\maroonD{5})-4\cdot\maroonD{5}^{-3}) = \ln\left(\dfrac52\right)-\dfrac{117}{250}$.

The answer:

$\displaystyle\int^{2}_{5}\left(\dfrac{12-x^3}{x^4}\right)\,dx ~=~\ln\left(\dfrac52\right)-\dfrac{117}{250}$

items.question_content:
The graph of $y=\sqrt{x}$ is shown below.

![The graph of the square root of x on the x y plane. It starts at (0, 0) and increases through the points (1, 1), (4, 2), and (9, 3).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c4d447646a783c7fc85a27e5d31d21541a7be7d7)

**Which of the following is the graph of $y=-\sqrt{x+3}-5$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The 
graph of $y=\sqrt{x}$ can be transformed
to get the graph of $y=-\sqrt{x+3}-5$. 
[[☃ explanation 1]]

Replacing $x$ with $x+3$ shifts the graph of $y=\sqrt{x}$ to the left $3$ units.

![The graph of two square root functions. One is the graph of the square root of x. It starts at (0, 0) and increases through the points (1, 1), (4, 2), and (9, 3). The other is the graph of the square root of x shifted to the left 3 units. It starts at (negative 3, 0) and increases through the points (negative 2, 1), (1, 2), and (6, 3). ](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c9124f621473c33e7eb4bd7c2bc7523717b72685)

Multiplying $\sqrt{x+3}$ by $-1$ reflects the graph of $y=\sqrt{x+3}$ over the $x$-axis.

![A square root function graph and its vertically reflected graph on an x y coordinate plane. It has an endpoint (negative 3, zero) and passes through (one, two). The vertically reflected graph has an endpoint (negative three, zero) and passes through (one, negative two).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8b582df7f8374048661d52543b3ed38ed1df2778)

Adding $-5$ to the function shifts the graph of $y=-\sqrt{x+3}$ down $5$ units.

![A square root function graph and its shifted graph on an x y coordinate plane. It has an endpoint (negative 3, zero) and passes through (one, negative two). The shifted graph has an endpoint (negative three, negative five) and passes through (one, negative seven).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/acb9d6efad3d717a391da666a7c1c8c4f2810257)

Note that it is important to follow order of operations when building the function, as function transformations are not always commutative.  [[☃ explanation 2]]

**The graph that corresponds with these transformations is graph $A$:**

![A square root function graph on an x y coordinate plane. It is shifted three units to the left, five units down and vertically reflected.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7746e7dec8e560e8a4924a211dca36e662d1e0cd)

Adding $3$ to the function shifts the graph of $y=2\sqrt{-(x+1)}$ up $3$ units.

[[☃ image 1]]

Note that it is important to follow order of operations when building the function, as function transformations are not always commutative.  [[☃ explanation 2]]

**The graph that corresponds with these transformations is graph $D$:**

![A cube root function graph on an x y coordinate plane. It has a middle point at (zero, zero) and passes through (negative eight, negative two) and (eight, two). ](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/afeb9a9d7e4c7c5089f71d0624a6146c529ef093)

**Which of the following is the graph of $y=3\sqrt[3]{x}+2$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{x}$ can be transformed
to get the graph of $y=3\sqrt[3]{x}+2$. 
[[☃ explanation 1]]

Multiplying $\sqrt[3]{x}$ by $3$ stretches the graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{x} $ vertically by a factor of $3$.  

[[☃ image 1]]

Note that graph of the function passes through $(8,2)$ *before* the stretch, and $(8, 6 )$ *after* the stretch.  This is because each $y$-coordinate is multiplied by $3$.

Adding $2$ to the function shifts the graph of $y=3\sqrt[3]{x}$ up $2$ units.

[[☃ image 1]]

Note that it is important to follow order of operations when building the function, as function transformations are not always commutative.  [[☃ explanation 1]]

**The graph that corresponds with these transformations is graph $D$:**

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following is the graph of $y=-2\sqrt[3]{x-3}+2$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{x}$ can be transformed
to get the graph of $y=-2\sqrt[3]{x-3}+2$. 
[[☃ explanation 1]]

Replacing $x$ with $x-3$ shifts the graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{x}$ right $3$ units.


[[☃ image 1]]

Multiplying $\sqrt[3]{x-3}$ by $-2$ reflects the graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{x-3}$ across the $x$-axis and stretches it vertically by a factor of $2$.

[[☃ image 1]]

Note that the graph of the function passes through $(2,-1)$ *before* the transformation, and $(2, 2 )$ *after* the transformation.  This is because each $y$-coordinate is multiplied by $-2$.

Adding $2$ to the function shifts the graph of $y=-2\sqrt[3]{x-3}$ up $2$ units.

[[☃ image 1]]

Note that it is important to follow order of operations when building the function, as function transformations are not always commutative.  [[☃ explanation 1]]

**The graph that corresponds with these transformations is graph $C$:**

[[☃ image 1]]

Adding $-5$ to the function shifts the graph of $y=-3\sqrt[3]{-x}$ down $5$ units.

**Which of the following is the graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{5-x}+4$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{x}$ can be transformed
to get the graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{5-x}+4$. 
[[☃ explanation 1]]

Let's write $y=\sqrt[3]{5-x}+4$ as $y=\sqrt[3]{-(x-5)}+4$ so that it is easier to see the transformations from the graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{x}$.  
Replacing $x$ with $x-5$ shifts the graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{x}$ right $5$ units.

[[☃ image 1]]

Multiplying $x-5$ by $-1$ reflects the graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{x-5}$ over the line $x=5$.


[[☃ image 1]]

Adding $4$ to the function shifts the graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{5-x}$ up $4$ units.


[[☃ image 1]]

Note that it is important to follow order of operations when building the function, as function transformations are not always commutative.  [[☃ explanation 1]]
**The graph that corresponds with these transformations is graph $B$:**


[[☃ image 1]]
